pick.rationale: Texas Tech holds significant analytical advantages with superior KenPom (19 vs 64) and Torvik (12 vs 68) rankings, featuring elite offensive efficiency (rank 9-12) that should challenge Akron's weaker defense (rank 99). While Akron has an impressive 28-5 record and MAC championship pedigree, their schedule strength and quality of competition pale in comparison to Texas Tech's Big 12 battles. The Red Raiders' Christian Anderson has been cleared to play after his injury scare, and their overall talent depth and tournament experience under Grant McCasland give them the edge in this classic 5/12 matchup despite their recent three-game losing streak.

reasoningStep.title: Texas Tech vs Akron Midwest 5/12 Matchup Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Texas Tech is the favored 5-seed with superior metrics, but Akron's strong record and MAC championship make them a credible upset threat in this traditional 5/12 upset-prone matchup.

Texas Tech entered tournament on 3-game losing streak but has elite Big 12 competition experience
Akron strength of schedule significantly weaker than Texas Tech's Big 12 slate

result.text: Is Texas Tech basketball's pre-March Madness losing skid troubling? Skip to main content Home NCAAB NCAAW MLB Women's Sports NFL NBA NCAAF WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Olympics Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers With 3 straight losses entering March Madness, is Texas Tech in trouble? Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal KANSAS CITY, Mo. — Nobody is going to pity Grant McCasland. He's handsomely paid to be the head coach of the Texas Tech basketball team at one of the most ambitious spending athletic departments in the country. But at the same time, you've gotta feel for the guy. McCasland has been the head coach at Tech for three full years now, spanning 103 total games . In that time, he's had his full arsenal available to him in just 16 games. Injuries have become second nature to McCasland that it seems he doesn't even flinch when another pops up. Half your rotation out for most of the year? Cool, let's set the program record for wins over Top 10 teams. Lose a national player of the year, Big 12 player of the year, and first team All-American candidate in one full swoop? Big whoop, we'll go into Ames and beat Iowa State. McCasland is an eternal optimist, always able to flip the negative into something deeper. It's part of what makes him such a great coach: finding joy in the struggle to reach the top of his profession. Even McCasland, though, has to think something's gotta give at some point. If it's not JT Toppin, having a historic season, tearing his ACL so close to the end of the season — after his team's bus tires were slashed after beating Arizona — it's seeing his other All-American, Christian Anderson, slipping and sliding all over the Big 12's insane ASP GlassFloor LED court , causing he and LeJuan Watts (perhaps an unrelated injury, perhaps related) to exit their Big 12 Tournament game with ailments. Compounding the current injury factor is the previous injuries to guys like Josiah Moseley, Luke Bamgboye and Marial Akuentok that set the team back from jump street. Before the season could even begin, the Red Raiders were shorthanded. It's the story of McCasland's existence at Texas Tech. Now is not the time to play the sympathy card, not that McCasland ever would. But he is a realist. As much as he wants to look at the bright side of things, he knows the Red Raiders and trending in the wrong direction and the worst possible time. Three straight losses to NCAA Tournament-bound teams isn't the end of the world. How they came about and what it says about his team's current state could be. The team has lacked poise under pressure, second-guessing every move th

result.text: Texas Tech vs. Akron: NCAA Tournament Picks & Odds | BettingPros to prevent flash of incorrect theme on page load --> Skip to main content Skip to navigation Skip to footer Click to go to homepage Picks Odds Tools Research Leaders Click to expand More menu Home Games Picks Upgrade Menu Click to Find a bet ... Contest Click to expand picks panel Click to open Sharp AI Texas Tech vs. Akron: NCAA Tournament Picks & Odds by BettingPros Staff | @BettingPros | Mar 16, 2026 by BettingPros Staff | @BettingPros | Mar 16, 2026 The 2026 NCAA Tournament is officially here! The March Madness Bracket is set, and first-round NCAA Tournament matchups are in place. It’s time to make your picks and predictions for the first round of the 2026 NCAA Tournament! We’re here to help as we’ll have picks and predictions for each of the first round 2026 NCAA Tournament games. Here are our NCAA Tournament picks and predictions for Texas Tech vs. Akron. NCAA Tournament Odds NCAA Tournament Pick’Em Contest NCAA Tournament March Madness Bracket Optimizer NCAA Tournament Betting Trends 2026 NCAA Tournament Picks & Predictions: Texas Tech vs. Akron Let’s dive into our top NCAA Tournament picks and predictions for this opening-round matchup. 2026 NCAA Tournament Preview: Texas Tech vs. Akron Texas Tech 2026 NCAA Tournament Preview JT Toppin tore his ACL at Arizona State on February 17th. After that game, the Red Raiders went 3-3. The Toppin loss is devastating in any evaluation of what Texas Tech could have been. And yet Grant McCasland has somehow kept his team at a level where it wouldn't be surprising if they made a run in March. That starts with Christian Anderson. The 6-foot-3 guard averages 19 points, leads the Big 12 in assists and is on the John R. Wooden Award watch list. Donovan Atwell set the Texas Tech program record for single-season three-pointers this season with 124 and ranks second nationally with 3.9 threes per game on 45.4% shooting. The Red Raiders led the Big 12 with 11.6 three-pointers per game and have made 10_ threes in 25 out of 32 games. There is a clear offensive identity for Texas Tech that is not easy to stop. Despite this team leaning on its perimeter shooting, the ceiling is seriously capped without its star. Across the whole season, without Toppin in the lineup, Texas Tech's defense dropped from 31st to 87th, and its turnover rate went from 16% to nearly 20%, 333rd in the country. The Red Raiders’ rim defense got worse, and their rebounding took a step back. Toppin was the only player on this roster who could legitimately rebound at an elite level against physical opponents, and his 10.8 per-game average is not something Luke Bamgboye, Lejuan Watts or Josiah Moseley can replicate. Anderson and Atwell are legitimate wrecking balls who are good enough to lift this team to some tournament wins. Texas Tech’s shooting can get hot enough to take down any team, and its perimeter defense doesn't leave much to

Skip to main content Home NCAAB NCAAW MLB Women's Sports NFL NBA NCAAF WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Olympics Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers With 3 straight losses entering March Madness, is Texas Tech in trouble? Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal KANSAS CITY, Mo. — Nobody is going to pity Grant McCasland. He's handsomely paid to be the head coach of the Texas Tech basketball team at one of the most ambitious spending athletic departments in the country. But at the same time, you've gotta feel for the guy. McCasland has been the head coach at Tech for three full years now, spanning 103 total games . In that time, he's had his full arsenal available to him in just 16 games. Injuries have become second nature to McCasland that it seems he doesn't even flinch when another pops up. Half your rotation out for most of the year? Cool, let's set the program record for wins over Top 10 teams. Lose a national player of the year, Big 12 player of the year, and first team All-American candidate in one full swoop? Big whoop, we'll go into Ames and beat Iowa State. McCasland is an eternal optimist, always able to flip the negative into something deeper. It's part of what makes him such a great coach: finding joy in the struggle to reach the top of his profession. Even McCasland, though, has to think something's gotta give at some point. If it's not JT Toppin, having a historic season, tearing his ACL so close to the end of the season — after his team's bus tires were slashed after beating Arizona — it's seeing his other All-American, Christian Anderson, slipping and sliding all over the Big 12's insane ASP GlassFloor LED court , causing he and LeJuan Watts (perhaps an unrelated injury, perhaps related) to exit their Big 12 Tournament game with ailments. Compounding the current injury factor is the previous injuries to guys like Josiah Moseley, Luke Bamgboye and Marial Akuentok that set the team back from jump street. Before the season could even begin, the Red Raiders were shorthanded. It's the story of McCasland's existence at Texas Tech. Now is not the time to play the sympathy card, not that McCasland ever would. But he is a realist. As much as he wants to look at the bright side of things, he knows the Red Raiders and trending in the wrong direction and the worst possible time. Three straight losses to NCAA Tournament-bound teams isn't the end of the world. How they came about and what it says about his team's current state could be. We’re here to help as we’ll have picks and predictions for each of the first round 2026 NCAA Tournament games. Here are our NCAA Tournament picks and predictions for Texas Tech vs. Akron. NCAA Tournament Odds NCAA Tournament Pick’Em Contest NCAA Tournament March Madness Bracket Optimizer NCAA Tournament Betting Trends 2026 NCAA Tournament Picks & Predictions: Texas Tech vs. Akron Let’s dive into our top NCAA Tournament picks and predictions for this opening-round matchup. 2026 NCAA Tournament Preview: Texas Tech vs. Akron Texas Tech 2026 NCAA Tournament Preview JT Toppin tore his ACL at Arizona State on February 17th. After that game, the Red Raiders went 3-3. The Toppin loss is devastating in any evaluation of what Texas Tech could have been. And yet Grant McCasland has somehow kept his team at a level where it wouldn't be surprising if they made a run in March. That starts with Christian Anderson. The 6-foot-3 guard averages 19 points, leads the Big 12 in assists and is on the John R. Wooden Award watch list. Donovan Atwell set the Texas Tech program record for single-season three-pointers this season with 124 and ranks second nationally with 3.9 threes per game on 45.4% shooting. The Red Raiders led the Big 12 with 11.6 three-pointers per game and have made 10_ threes in 25 out of 32 games. There is a clear offensive identity for Texas Tech that is not easy to stop. Despite this team leaning on its perimeter shooting, the ceiling is seriously capped without its star. Across the whole season, without Toppin in the lineup, Texas Tech's defense dropped from 31st to 87th, and its turnover rate went from 16% to nearly 20%, 333rd in the country. The Red Raiders’ rim defense got worse, and their rebounding took a step back. Toppin was the only player on this roster who could legitimately rebound at an elite level against physical opponents, and his 10.8 per-game average is not something Luke Bamgboye, Lejuan Watts or Josiah Moseley can replicate. Anderson and Atwell are legitimate wrecking balls who are good enough to lift this team to some tournament wins.
